patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious office manager. Speak tangentially, jumping between symptoms, work stress, and money worries. Start with: “I can’t stop having watery diarrhea, and I feel totally drained.” Volunteer early: months of frequent watery “like water” diarrhea, intense thirst/exhaustion, weight loss (“food runs right through me”), crampy belly, and brief face flushing. If asked, add: diarrhea even when not eating, wakes you at night, lightheaded on standing, leg cramps/weakness, intermittent nausea, dry mouth/peeing less on bad days, occasional racing heart when dehydrated, and burning “sour stomach” after meals. Do NOT volunteer unless directly asked: heavy OTC anti-diarrheals/electrolyte drinks, bathroom avoidance/missed work, tingling/twitching, salty cravings, easy bruising/nosebleeds worse lately. Resist admitting accidents and low libido unless asked gently; then disclose with embarrassment. Misconceptions: suspect “allergic swelling” (puffy lips/eyelids, hand/foot swelling), blame a “bleeding condition,” and mention online thyroid/food intolerance; say dairy/gluten cuts didn’t help. Emphasize cost: want pre-approval, prefer cheaper pills, minimize visits. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Tranexamic acid 650 mg PO three times daily as needed for epistaxis
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) with recurrent epistaxis
confounders: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia with chronic blood loss causing anemia, fatigue, and tachycardia that can mimic dehydration-related symptoms
Pantoprazole use may obscure gastric acid findings (VIPoma classically associated with achlorhydria)
Recent intermittent use of over-the-counter magnesium-containing antacids reported by patient (can contribute to diarrhea and electrolyte abnormalities)
Possible small-bowel angiodysplasia/AVMs from HHT causing GI symptoms and complicating attribution of weight loss and weakness

correct_diagnosis.primary: VIPoma (vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) causing WDHA syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with VIP hypersecretion
WDHA syndrome (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria) due to VIPoma
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum VIP (typically >200 pg/mL) in the setting of secretory watery diarrhea
Electrolyte pattern consistent with secretory diarrhea: hypokalemia and non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (low HCO3-) with hyperchloremia
Clinical dehydration/volume depletion with high-volume watery diarrhea that persists with fasting
Supportive findings may include hypomagnesemia and hyperglycemia

expected_lab_results.Serum VIP: Elevated (classically >200 pg/mL fasting; often markedly high)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (HCO3-): Low (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss in stool)
expected_lab_results.Chloride (Cl-): High or high-normal (hyperchloremia consistent with non-anion gap acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Sodium (Na+): Low-normal to low (can be low with volume depletion; may be normal depending on replacement)
expected_lab_results.Magnesium (Mg2+): Low (hypomagnesemia may occur with chronic diarrhea)
expected_lab_results.Calcium (total): Normal (may be mildly elevated if MEN1/primary hyperparathyroidism coexists, but not required for diagnosis)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): High or high-normal (VIP can cause hyperglycemia)

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 1-2 L then maintenance per vitals/urine output
Potassium chloride IV 10-20 mEq/hour with continuous ECG monitoring (or PO if mild and tolerating), titrate to K+ >3.5 mEq/L
Magnesium sulfate IV 1-2 g (8-16 mEq) over 1 hour, repeat as needed to normalize Mg2+
Octreotide 100-200 mcg subcutaneous every 8 hours (titrate to control diarrhea; consider continuous IV infusion if severe)
If persistent/refractory or for long-term control: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks (after response to short-acting formulation)
non_pharmacological: Strict intake/output monitoring, daily weights, and frequent electrolyte checks until stable

Evaluate for tumor localization and staging after stabilization (e.g., pancreatic imaging and somatostatin receptor imaging) and refer to surgical oncology/endocrinology for definitive management (resection if localized; systemic options if metastatic)
Avoid empiric antibiotics unless there is evidence of infectious diarrhea
Consider MEN1 evaluation if clinical suspicion (e.g., assess calcium for hyperparathyroidism; family history review)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess electrolytes and clinical status within 4-6 hours during acute stabilization; specialty follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge for definitive tumor management planning.

must_include: The diarrhea is likely secretory and driven by excess VIP hormone
This causes large fluid losses with low potassium and acid-base/electrolyte disturbances
Further imaging is needed to find and stage the tumor and guide definitive treatment
Immediate priority is IV fluids and electrolyte replacement to prevent heart rhythm and kidney complications
Octreotide reduces VIP release and typically improves diarrhea
Definitive treatment often involves tumor-directed therapy (surgery if possible; other options if spread)
Review penicillin allergy and clarify reaction details
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care (worsening weakness, palpitations, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, decreased urination)
Set expectations that symptoms may recur without ongoing tumor control and follow-up is essential
Hydration plan and when to use oral rehydration solutions
Avoid dehydration triggers and monitor stool volume if possible
Adherence to follow-up labs and appointments for electrolyte monitoring and tumor evaluation

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to characterize diarrhea (secretory features), severity, dehydration, red flags, medication exposures, and neuroendocrine tumor clues; confirm allergy history and pregnancy status where relevant.

Order initial evaluation for secretory diarrhea and complications: BMP/CMP (K, HCO3, Mg, creatinine), venous/arterial blood gas if severe, CBC, serum glucose, stool electrolytes/osmotic gap if available; include VIP level if benchmark allows; consider ECG for hypokalemia.
Interpret labs for hypokalemia, non–anion gap metabolic acidosis, dehydration/AKI, and findings consistent with secretory diarrhea; confirm/strengthen suspicion for VIPoma.
Make and communicate working diagnosis: VIPoma causing secretory diarrhea with electrolyte/acid-base disturbances; note need for tumor localization and staging imaging.
Perform required critical safety check prior to prescribing; specifically clarify penicillin allergy and reaction details.
Perform non-critical interaction screen prior to prescribing (e.g., octreotide effects with glucose-lowering meds; QT risks with severe electrolyte derangements).

Initiate treatment: urgent IV fluids and electrolyte replacement (especially potassium, magnesium) and start octreotide to reduce VIP-mediated secretion; consider antiemetics and inpatient management if severe.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and treatment priorities; address concerns and warning signs; provide hydration/lifestyle guidance and expectations about recurrence without tumor control.

Arrange close follow-up for repeat electrolytes, symptom monitoring, and expedited imaging/tumor evaluation (CT/MRI pancreas, somatostatin receptor imaging) and specialty referral (endocrinology/oncology/surgery).
Close encounter after safety-netting, confirming understanding, and ensuring follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female project coordinator. You open anxious and a bit scattered, jumping between details. Start with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with everything.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep, waking a lot, daytime irritability and exhaustion. If asked, add it’s been months; racing work thoughts at bedtime; clock-watching; only 4–5 broken hours; afternoon coffee; OTC sleep aid helps little; missed work from exhaustion.

Only reveal later/if specifically asked: you scroll on your phone in bed 1–2 hours, nap after work (1+ hour), drink 2 evening drinks to “take the edge off,” little sunlight/exercise, inconsistent weekday/weekend schedule.

Resist discussing mood (“I’m fine, just tired”) and minimize cannabis use; feel embarrassed about loud snoring/possible pauses in breathing—admit only if directly asked. Express misconceptions: think it’s hormones/thyroid, maybe ADHD, fear a brain problem; melatonin didn’t fix it so something is “really wrong.” Worry CBT-I/therapy and sleep testing are expensive/time-consuming and you want the cheapest pill. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, say you’re overwhelmed and want practical steps that won’t risk your job.

medications: Aprobarbital 200 mg by mouth at bedtime as needed for sleep
Sertraline 50 mg by mouth once daily
Amlodipine 5 mg by mouth once daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth every other day
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU by mouth once daily
comorbidities: Iron deficiency (likely from heavy menstrual bleeding)
confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms with nocturnal hot flashes
Excess caffeine intake (3-4 cups coffee/day, last cup late afternoon)
Evening alcohol use (1-2 glasses wine most nights) worsening sleep continuity
Possible restless legs symptoms related to low ferritin
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.1, reports snoring)
SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline taken in the evening)
Shift in sleep schedule due to late-night screen use and irregular bedtime

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Present for at least 3 months (or specify short-term insomnia if <3 months)
Clinically significant daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, concentration/mood disturbance, reduced performance)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome), substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (rules out overt thyroid dysfunction contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (low ferritin would raise concern for restless legs syndrome contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low or insufficient but not diagnostic for insomnia; treat if deficient per guidelines
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range or stable; uncontrolled diabetes/nocturia would be an alternative contributor

medications: Avoid initiating Aprobarbital; if already taking, begin supervised taper per prescriber to reduce dependence/withdrawal risk
Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly as needed for sleep-maintenance insomnia
Ramelteon 8 mg PO nightly for sleep-onset insomnia
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)

Sleep hygiene: consistent sleep/wake schedule, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, limit screens/bright light 1-2 hours before bed, keep bedroom dark/quiet/cool, use bed only for sleep/sex
Address contributors: review all substances/medications (stimulants, decongestants, corticosteroids), manage pain, GERD, nocturia, anxiety/depression as indicated

Screen for obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness) and restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening); refer for sleep study or treat iron deficiency if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess response, adherence to CBT-I components, side effects, and need for further evaluation/referral

must_include: Define insomnia and confirm pattern/frequency/duration and daytime impact
Explain that labs help rule out contributing medical issues but insomnia is diagnosed clinically
Discuss common contributors (stress, schedule, substances, sleep apnea, restless legs, mood disorders)
CBT-I is first-line and has durable benefit compared with sedatives

If medication is used, aim for lowest effective dose, short duration, and reassess regularly

Order targeted labs only if clinically indicated to rule out contributing medical issues (e.g., TSH for hyperthyroid symptoms, CBC/ferritin if restless legs suspected, CMP if concern for systemic illness/med safety).

Make a clinical diagnosis of insomnia after confirming criteria and excluding urgent alternate explanations; document subtype and likely contributors.

Explain diagnosis, first-line CBT-I, sleep hygiene, stimulus control, and sleep restriction; address patient goals/concerns and shared decision-making; include safety plan regarding sedation and mental health escalation.
Critical safety check immediately before any prescribing; confirm medication and class allergies and prior adverse reactions.
Critical safety check immediately before any prescribing; review current meds/substances (including alcohol, cannabis, OTC antihistamines) for additive CNS depression or CYP interactions.

Critical safety check immediately before any prescribing; assess contraindications (pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, untreated sleep apnea/respiratory disease, high fall risk, older age considerations).

If medication is chosen after shared decision-making, prescribe a guideline-concordant option at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration with clear instructions; explicitly avoid barbiturates (e.g., Aprobarbital) for insomnia due to dependence/overdose/withdrawal risk.
Arrange follow-up to reassess sleep, adverse effects, adherence to CBT-I components, and taper/stop any hypnotic; earlier follow-up if mood worsens or safety concerns arise.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Retrieve enzyme and genetic results needed to support Sandhoff diagnosis (both total hexosaminidase and Hex A reduced; pathogenic/likely pathogenic HEXB variants).
Make/confirm diagnosis of Sandhoff disease and phenotype (infantile vs juvenile/late-onset) based on onset and clinical course plus enzyme/genetic results.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain supportive treatment approach, address concerns (including genetics/recurrence risk and resources), and educate lifestyle/safety (feeding/aspiration, seizure safety, infection prevention, medication monitoring).
Critical safety check before any prescribing for symptom management (e.g., antiseizure meds, antispasticity agents, secretion control).

Critical safety check before prescribing: assess respiratory status, swallowing/aspiration risk, hepatic/renal impairment, and other condition-specific contraindications relevant to sedating agents/anticholinergics/benzodiazepines.
Non-critical but expected: check interactions with current meds (e.g., polytherapy antiseizure meds, sedatives) before adding symptom-directed prescriptions.

If clinically indicated, prescribe symptom-directed therapy (e.g., antiseizure regimen/rescue plan, spasticity management, secretion control, constipation prophylaxis) using start-low/titrate and clear adverse-effect/urgent-care counseling.
Arrange referrals and follow-up: genetics/metabolic, neurology, palliative care, nutrition/SLP swallow evaluation, respiratory/pulmonology as needed; set timeline and emergency plan review.
Close after verifying understanding (teach-back), confirming plan, and providing return precautions.

nl_assertions: Explains Sandhoff disease as an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by HEXB-related beta-hexosaminidase deficiency leading to GM2 accumulation.
Accurately interprets diagnostic testing: both total hexosaminidase and Hex A reduced on enzyme assay, with confirmatory pathogenic/likely pathogenic HEXB variants on genetic testing.
Describes phenotype variability (infantile vs juvenile/late-onset) and provides an honest prognosis without false reassurance, while acknowledging uncertainty and focusing on goals of care.
Explicitly states the condition is not caused by caregiver actions and is not contagious.
States there is no curative standard therapy currently and frames management as supportive and symptom-directed with goals of comfort and function.

Reviews any proposed medications with purpose, start-low/titrate approach, common side effects (sedation, constipation, urinary retention, respiratory suppression, worsening swallowing/aspiration risk), and clear triggers for urgent evaluation.
Invites caregiver priorities and goals (quality of life, feeding, breathing, comfort, seizures) and aligns the plan accordingly.
Covers genetic counseling: 25% recurrence risk each pregnancy if both parents are carriers; offers carrier testing for parents/relatives and options for prenatal or preimplantation genetic testing.
Provides resources and support options (social work, palliative care, patient advocacy groups) and gives emergency guidance for seizures, aspiration, and respiratory distress.

Gives lifestyle/safety education: aspiration precautions and feeding guidance per swallow evaluation, seizure home safety and rescue plan if applicable, infection prevention/respiratory hygiene, and medication adherence/monitoring.
Performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check and contraindication check are completed; interaction check is attempted or explicitly deferred with rationale.
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., initiating sedating/respiratory-depressant meds without assessing baseline respiratory/swallowing risk; advising unproven curative therapies as standard of care).
Uses clear, compassionate language, checks understanding (teach-back), and documents follow-up/referral plan.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm Sandhoff disease with enzyme/genetic results, deliver required counseling milestones, and create a supportive-care/referral plan; prescribe only if symptom burden warrants and after safety checks.
Same as minimal path, plus symptom-directed prescribing (e.g., seizure rescue plan or secretion/spasticity management) with mandatory allergy and contraindication checks and interaction review.

acceptable_variants.description: If diagnosis is already established with documented enzyme and HEXB results, the agent may skip ordering labs and instead verify results via GET_RESULTS before DIAGNOSE and counseling.

If no medications are prescribed (supportive counseling/referrals only), safety checks are not required; the agent should explicitly state no prescriptions are being initiated today and provide return precautions.

If urgent uncontrolled seizures/respiratory compromise is identified during ASK, the agent may prioritize emergency guidance and expedited referral/transfer before completing full education, then complete milestones as feasible.
Interaction check may occur immediately after medication reconciliation (post-ASK) and before allergy/contraindication checks, as long as all required checks occur before PRESCRIBE.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, an anxious 11-year-old who speaks vaguely and worries something serious is happening. Start with: “My wrists and ankles have been hurting and swelling, and my hands look like they’re changing shape.” If asked to explain, volunteer: aching pain in both wrists/ankles for months, swelling that comes and goes, weak grip, fingers look shorter, wrists look bonier. Only if asked, add: worse with use/end of day, rest helps; morning stiffness usually <1 hour; feet hurt walking/avoiding stairs; wrist clicking/instability; flatter feet/shoes fit differently; sometimes foamy urine; puffy face/eyes mornings; higher BP at pharmacy kiosk. If pressed about school, reluctantly admit you’ve skipped school because you’re scared of people noticing your hands, and you’re using more pain pills than you want to admit because you can’t sleep. Bring up your misconception: you fear Zika after a trip with rash/red eyes/low fever months ago. Repeatedly worry about cost and ask for “one test” instead of many; doubt PT value. required_evidence: Progressive osteolysis predominantly involving carpal and tarsal bones leading to deformity/functional impairment
Onset typically in childhood with non-inflammatory appearing joint swelling/pain and progressive bone loss on imaging (radiographs/MRI)
Laboratory inflammatory markers often normal or only mildly elevated relative to severity of bony destruction (ESR/CRP may be normal)
Absence of systemic infectious prodrome (fever, conjunctivitis, diffuse rash) that would suggest acute arboviral illness

Family history or genetic confirmation (e.g., MAFB pathogenic variant) if available/ordered

expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal or mildly elevated (often not commensurate with degree of joint/bone damage)
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Typically normal; may be elevated if significant renal impairment is present

Avoid NSAIDs given ibuprofen allergy; avoid empiric antibiotics unless clear infection

Need for renal monitoring and nephrology involvement if abnormalities found

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia history (onset, duration, frequency, sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), daytime impairment, precipitating factors, and current self-treatments (including phenacetin) to establish problem definition and urgency.

Make a clear insomnia diagnosis and classify as short-term vs chronic based on duration/frequency; document subtype (sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance) and contributing factors.

Address concerns: specifically ask about phenacetin use and counsel to stop due to serious toxicity and no role in insomnia; review penicillin allergy (hives) and reassure typical insomnia meds are not limited; reinforce red-flag plan if present.
Before any prescribing, confirm/document allergies and reaction type to prevent avoidable adverse events (critical safety check).
Before prescribing, check for interactions with current medications/substances (including alcohol, sedatives, opioids, antihistamines) (critical safety check).

Before prescribing, assess contraindications (e.g., untreated sleep apnea risk, severe hepatic impairment, pregnancy considerations, history of complex sleep behaviors with hypnotics, narrow-angle glaucoma/urinary retention risk for anticholinergic agents) (critical safety check).

If medication is appropriate as adjunct: choose based on subtype (melatonin/ramelteon for sleep-onset; low-dose doxepin for sleep-maintenance), use lowest effective dose, time-limited when possible, and counsel on common side effects (next-day sedation, dizziness).

Provide lifestyle and safety counseling: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol timing, screen/blue light reduction, consistent schedule, and driving/operating machinery cautions especially after starting sedating meds.
Arrange follow-up to assess response and adherence (CBT-I progress, side effects, ongoing red flags), and to adjust plan; earlier follow-up if safety concerns.
Close encounter with clear plan, return precautions, and documentation of counseling and safety checks.

No better alternative diagnosis explaining the presentation at time of decision-making

expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute lymphocyte count: Low (lymphopenia) is common in severe influenza/avian influenza
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal (unless baseline anemia or bleeding)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to low (mild thrombocytopenia can occur in severe viral infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Normal or mildly low (possible hyponatremia with severe illness)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Usually normal; may vary with dehydration or renal impairment

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate reassessment within 4-6 hours if outpatient considered; otherwise inpatient monitoring with daily reassessment (earlier if worsening).

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Length-dependent, slowly progressive distal weakness and atrophy (feet/legs > hands), often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood
Distal sensory loss (vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (high arches/pes cavus, hammertoes) and/or frequent ankle sprains/steppage gait
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (autosomal dominant most common) OR longstanding symptoms without systemic features
Screening labs for acquired neuropathy are not suggestive of diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, inflammatory myopathy, or systemic inflammation (A1c/glucose, B12/MMA, TSH, CK, ESR/CRP)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated (not consistent with primary myositis/rhabdomyolysis)

non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmation and phenotyping (nerve conduction studies/EMG) and discussion of genetic testing and family counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and fall-risk reduction
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/adaptive devices as needed
Orthotics: ankle-foot orthoses for foot drop; custom insoles; supportive footwear
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for pes cavus/hammertoes; consider surgical correction if severe or refractory
Home safety and fall-prevention measures; consider assistive devices (cane/walker) if instability

Avoid/limit medications with known peripheral neuropathy risk when alternatives exist (e.g., vincristine, amiodarone, metronidazole, nitrofurantoin, isoniazid without pyridoxine, linezolid, excessive alcohol)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and medication tolerability in 4-6 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if rapid worsening, new weakness, or falls

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness, sensory loss, and foot deformities
Course is typically gradual; severity varies by subtype; life expectancy usually normal
Reason for labs: to rule out common acquired/treatable neuropathy causes (diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, inflammation, myopathy)
No cure currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, symptom control, and preventing complications
Role of PT/OT and orthotics (AFOs) to improve gait and reduce falls
Neuropathic pain plan and titration/side effects (sedation, dizziness) and safety precautions (driving, fall risk)
Discuss inheritance and offer genetic counseling/testing options and implications for family members
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: rapid progression, new asymmetry, bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe proximal weakness, or systemic symptoms
Shared decision-making about referrals and goals (mobility, pain control, work/school accommodations)

Fall-prevention strategies and exercise within tolerance (low-impact aerobic + strengthening)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 12-year-old girl. You are anxious and speak vaguely, often saying “I’m not sure” and looking to your parent for help. Start with: “My wrists and ankles have been hurting and getting weaker, and my hands look like they’re changing shape.” Volunteer: months of on-and-off swelling/pain in both wrists getting worse, aching ankles/feet worse after walking, weaker grip/dropping things, fingers look shorter/pulled in and wrists look bony, OTC pain meds don’t help much. cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
comorbidities: Chronic kidney disease (likely hereditary nephropathy associated with multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis)
Mild normocytic anemia (anemia of chronic disease/CKD)
confounders: Intermittent joint swelling and hand pain previously labeled as juvenile idiopathic arthritis despite normal inflammatory markers
Low BMI and vitamin D deficiency contributing to bone pain and fragility, potentially obscuring primary osteolysis
ACE inhibitor therapy can increase serum potassium and creatinine, complicating interpretation of renal labs

diagnosis.primary: multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy (MCTO)
acceptable_alternatives: Multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA syndrome)
Osteogenesis imperfecta (less likely if osteolysis pattern is classic)
Septic arthritis/osteomyelitis (if acute systemic features present)
required_evidence: Progressive osteolysis predominantly affecting carpal and/or tarsal bones (classically radiographic finding), often presenting in childhood
Clinical course with pain/swelling, deformity, and loss of function in hands/feet, with progressive joint destruction rather than erosive synovitis pattern
Inflammatory markers may be normal or mildly elevated; lack of clear infectious source
Assessment for nephropathy: history/physical suggestive of hypertension/edema and plan to screen renal involvement (urinalysis and renal function not provided in current labs)
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant pattern or known MAFB mutation (if available), or sporadic case with classic phenotype

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation is present, but anemia is not a defining feature
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal; leukocytosis would suggest infection or alternative inflammatory process
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal; thrombocytosis may occur with inflammation but is nonspecific
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal to mildly elevated (not typically markedly elevated)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal to mildly elevated (marked elevation suggests infection/CRMO/systemic inflammatory disease)
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Usually normal; abnormalities would raise concern for renal involvement or other comorbidity

medications: Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO every 4-6 hours as needed for pain (max 75 mg/kg/day; do not exceed 4 g/day)

Naproxen 5-7.5 mg/kg PO twice daily with food as needed for pain/inflammation (ONLY if history suggests isolated ibuprofen allergy without cross-reactivity; otherwise avoid NSAIDs and use acetaminophen)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric rheumatology/orthopedics for confirmation (imaging review) and longitudinal management; consider genetics referral/testing (MAFB) if available

Screen for nephropathy: order urinalysis with protein quantification (urine protein/creatinine ratio), serum creatinine/BUN, eGFR, and blood pressure measurement; nephrology referral if proteinuria, hematuria, hypertension, or impaired renal function
Hand/foot occupational and physical therapy: joint protection strategies, range-of-motion maintenance, strengthening within pain limits
Splinting/orthotics for wrists/ankles/feet as indicated to reduce pain and slow deformity progression
Activity modification: avoid high-impact activities that worsen pain; encourage low-impact conditioning
Assistive devices as needed (supports, adaptive tools) and school/work accommodations
Avoid empiric immunosuppression (e.g., DMARDs/biologics) unless an inflammatory arthritis alternative diagnosis is established
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Specialist follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (within 1 week) if significant functional decline, severe pain, or any renal warning signs (edema, hypertension, decreased urine output)

must_include: This condition causes progressive breakdown (osteolysis) of specific bones in the hands/feet (carpal/tarsal), leading to pain, deformity, and functional limits
It can be mistaken for inflammatory arthritis, but typical arthritis medicines may not help unless another diagnosis is present
Some patients can develop kidney involvement (nephropathy), so kidney screening is an important part of care
Focus is on pain control, protecting joints, maintaining function (PT/OT, splints/orthotics), and monitoring complications
Plan for imaging/genetics/specialist confirmation and renal screening
Clear medication instructions and limits (acetaminophen maximum daily dose; NSAID use only if safe given allergy history and renal status)
Review allergy history: ibuprofen caused urticaria (hives) and penicillin rash as infant; discuss what medications to avoid and what alternatives are safe

Red flags requiring urgent care: fever with severe joint pain, rapidly worsening swelling/redness, inability to bear weight/use hand, new swelling/edema, high blood pressure symptoms, decreased urine output
Set expectations: chronic condition requiring ongoing specialist follow-up; goal is symptom control and function preservation
Joint protection and activity modification (low-impact exercise, avoid repetitive high-stress activities)
Use of splints/orthotics and adaptive tools to reduce pain and prevent injury
Importance of attending renal screening and blood pressure checks even if feeling well
